o describe   in short   compass   the ook   of noch   is impossible.   —
t   comes   from   many   writers   and   almost   as   many   periods,   t
touches   upon   every   subject   that could   have arisen   in the ancient
schools   of the prophets, but naturally it deals   with   these   subjects
in   an   advanced   stage   of development.   early   every   religious
idea   appears   in a variety of   forms,   and, if these   are   studied   in   ~
relation   to their contets   and dates, we cannot fail to observe   that
in the age to which   the noch   literature   belongs   there   is move-
ment   everywhere,   and   nowhere   dogmatic   fiity   and   finality.
nd   though   at times   the movement   may   be reactionary,   yet the
general   trend   is onward   and   upward.   n   fact   the   history   of   |
the development   of the higher   theology during the two   centuries
before   the hristian   era   could   not   be written   without   the   ook
of noch.
